title: Concatenation of Array
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single recommended solution is also the speed extreme and the memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.cpp is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file. A C malloc+memcpy version does identical machine work (output-dominated footprint), so it is not a distinct memory champion.

variants.languageReason: Task is pure contiguous int copying. reserve(2n) gives one exact-size allocation (no reallocation), vector::insert from contiguous iterators lowers to memmove (one bulk copy per half), and vector<int> is the native return type (NRVO/move, no buffer copy). C ties on the memcpy hot path and is leaner only by a vector header, but the footprint is dominated by the mandatory 2n-int output, so the axes coincide and the readable native C++ proposal carries the single file. Top 1% on both axes.
variants.approachJustification: ans[i]==nums[i] and ans[i+n]==nums[i] is exactly nums concatenated with itself. Size the output once at 2n and bulk-copy nums twice, avoiding reallocation, zero-fill, and per-element push_back overhead. Also serves as the speed and memory extreme.

reasoningSummary: Return nums concatenated with itself. The 2n-int output must be fully written (Omega(n) lower bound) and needs no auxiliary structure, so the problem is Pareto-optimal. reserve(2n) allocates the exact final size once and two bulk inserts (memmove for trivially copyable int) fill it, beating push_back loops (reallocations + per-element checks), copy-construct-then-append (extra reallocation), and vector(2n)-then-overwrite (redundant zero-fill). O(n) time and O(1) auxiliary space are both optimal, so a single file ships.
